national_expectations.summary: Argentina enter the Round of 32 as overwhelming favourites and one of the tournament's leading contenders, having topped Group J with a perfect record and conceded only once. Reigning world champions, they are widely expected to beat tournament debutants Cape Verde comfortably in Miami, with national coverage framing the tie as a stepping stone toward a deeper knockout run and a bid to defend the title won in 2022. Previews still stress that Cape Verde troubled stronger sides in the group stage, so the expectation is a win earned with respect rather than a foregone formality.

national_mood.summary: The mood around the national team is buoyant and confident after a flawless group stage capped by a 3-1 win over Jordan, with Lionel Messi's continued scoring fuelling optimism and emotion inside the camp. Teammates spoke of being energised by watching Messi every day, while the squad's depth and ability to rotate has reinforced a sense of a side peaking at the right time. Beneath the optimism, the messaging from the camp carries deliberate caution about the sharper margins of knockout football.

manager_situation.summary: Lionel Scaloni is secure and in full command, his standing strengthened by the perfect group campaign. He used the final group match to rotate, resting Messi from the start against Jordan and stressing the choice was not dictated by the next opponent. Ahead of the Cape Verde tie he has set a cautious, respectful tone, repeatedly warning his players not to underestimate a side that troubled Spain, Uruguay and Saudi Arabia, while framing the knockout phase as effectively a fresh, different competition where rest is shorter and injuries can end a tournament.

historical_context.summary: Argentina arrive as reigning world champions, having won the 2022 World Cup and added the 2024 Copa America. They are chasing a rare back-to-back World Cup, an achievement no nation has managed since Brazil in 1958 and 1962 (Italy did it in 1934 and 1938). For Messi, in his sixth World Cup, the campaign offers the chance to win something even Diego Maradona never did: defend the trophy. The Round of 32 meeting in Miami pits the holders against Cape Verde, tournament debutants and the smallest country by population to reach a men's World Cup knockout round.

dominant_storylines.storyline: Perfect group, Messi milestones: Argentina won Group J with three wins and only one goal conceded, finishing with a 3-1 victory over Jordan in which Messi scored off the bench to extend his men's all-time World Cup goal record and became the first man to score in seven straight World Cup matches.

Respect, not complacency: Scaloni has publicly cautioned against underestimating Cape Verde, pointing out the islanders troubled Spain, Uruguay and Saudi Arabia, and has framed the knockouts as a 'different World Cup' with sharper margins.

Title-defence history bid: As reigning champions and 2024 Copa America winners, Argentina are chasing a back-to-back World Cup no team has achieved since Brazil in 1958 and 1962, with Messi eyeing a feat Maradona never managed.

Holders vs Cinderella: The tie is widely framed as the reigning champions against tournament debutants Cape Verde, the smallest nation ever to reach a men's World Cup knockout round, staged at Hard Rock Stadium in Miami.

Miami homecoming / 'playing as locals': Argentina's own press frames the Cape Verde tie at Hard Rock Stadium as a virtual home game, with the large Argentine diaspora around Miami mobilising, soaring resale ticket prices and a planned flag-raising on the eve of the match feeding a euphoric, festive mood around Messi's side.

Anti-triumphalism mood: National outlet La Nacion explicitly frames the camp as refusing to ride the 'triumphalist wave' and eyeing Cape Verde warily, with senior players invoking the 2022 scare against Saudi Arabia as a cautionary tale to keep the squad grounded despite being heavy favourites.

Positionless attack debate: La Nacion's match analysis of the Jordan win highlights Scaloni experimenting with a fluid, almost position-less front-line in the group finale (Palacios at right-back, Nicolas Paz rotating between interior, playmaker and forward, a Lautaro-Julian pairing reserved for when Messi is absent) as a knockout-stage testing ground.

attributed_quotes.quote: Cape Verde didn't surprise me, because it's a good team. It's a tough opponent.
attributed_quotes.context: Speaking after the 3-1 win over Jordan, warning his squad against underestimating their Round of 32 opponent.

attributed_quotes.quote: We achieved the first goal, which is to advance to the next round, and now we're going for more.
attributed_quotes.context: Speaking after group qualification, framing the knockout run ahead before preparing for Cape Verde.

attributed_quotes.quote: Cape Verde got the results it got for a reason. It competed; it's an aggressive team.
attributed_quotes.quote_original: Cabo Verde por algo obtuvo los resultados que logró. Compitió, es un equipo agresivo
attributed_quotes.context: Warning teammates against complacency ahead of the Round of 32 tie, in a piece framing Argentina's refusal to get carried away.

attributed_quotes.quote: Saudi Arabia was a so-called easy match and we ended up suffering.
attributed_quotes.quote_original: Arabia Saudí era un partido entre comillas fácil y terminamos sufriendo
attributed_quotes.context: Recalling the 2022 group-stage upset by Saudi Arabia as a cautionary tale against underestimating Cape Verde.

attributed_quotes.quote: No, I told him Nico Paz was coming off and he was coming on. Nothing more.
attributed_quotes.quote_original: No, le dije que salía Nico Paz y entraba él. Nada más.
attributed_quotes.context: Scaloni's bemused reply when asked what tactical instructions he gave Messi before bringing him off the bench against Jordan.

local_availability_reporting.status_reported: Managed minutes; reported to have been dealing with a minor left hamstring issue before the tournament and was rested from the start against Jordan, coming off the bench to score. No fresh injury concern reported for the Cape Verde tie.

gaps: No official FIFA / AFA (Argentine Football Association) federation statement sourced for any section; all material is press/media (discovery tier).
No confirmed Argentina starting lineup or official team-news bulletin for the Cape Verde match found; only press rotation reporting from the group finale.
No verified injury/suspension list or official medical update for the Round of 32 sourced; Messi's hamstring mention is pre-tournament reporting, not a current confirmed status.

Buenos Aires Herald primary article could not be fetched directly (HTTP 403); its content ('a different World Cup begins'; knockout caution) was corroborated via search snippet, so exact in-article quotes beyond that phrase were not independently re-read in full.
Texarkana Gazette (AP) article 403'd on direct re-fetch; the Cape Verde 'smallest nation'/'debutants' claim was corroborated via ESPN and Al Jazeera and an ESPN URL substituted as the primary source.
Limited Spanish-language national-press sourcing (e.g., Ole, TyC Sports, La Nacion) captured; mood read leans on English-language outlets and an Inter Miami club source.
No fan/public sentiment reporting from Argentina (street/celebration coverage) independently sourced beyond camp-internal player quotes.
Venue/date now resolved to Hard Rock Stadium, Miami on July 3 (ESPN and Buenos Aires Herald agree).
